metadata.title: The Polypticum which is called Perpendiculum (alternative recension)
metadata.author: Atto of Vercelli
known_issues: The translation prioritizes readability over perfect fidelity given the source's deliberate obscurity

The work appears to be a satirical critique of political machinations, corruption among nobility, and ecclesiastical affairs, but the allegorical and rhetorical complexity makes definitive interpretation difficult

This text contains extremely obscure and convoluted Latin with numerous rare words, neologisms, and tortured syntax characteristic of the Hermeneutic style popular in tenth-century intellectual circles
References to historical figures (Saul, Manasseh, Ezekiel, Licinius, Constantine, Theodosius) mix biblical and Roman imperial history
The concluding mystical prayer section becomes particularly cryptic
Many passages are nearly incomprehensible even in Latin, mixing classical terms with medieval vocabulary and deliberate obscurity
Several Greek transliterations appear in the original (homoousios is mentioned in instructions but not present in this text fragment)
The 'alternative recension' designation suggests textual variants exist; this translation is based solely on the PL134 version

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: POLYPTICI TEXTUS ALTER Editus a Joanne Dominico MANSI in Miscellaneis sacris Baluzii, edit. Luc., p. 560. INCIPIT POLYPTICUM QUOD APPELLATUR PERPENDICULUM.
chunks.english: Here begins the Polypticum, which is called the Perpendiculum. This alternative text was published by Giovanni Domenico Mansi in the sacred miscellany of Baluze, Lucca edition, page five hundred sixty.
chunks.notes: The title 'Perpendiculum' means 'plumb line' or 'measuring standard,' suggesting the work serves as a moral or political standard for judgment.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: INCIPIT EPISTOLA. Reverendo in me Fulano valde praesuli.
chunks.english: Here begins the letter. To the very reverend bishop, so-and-so, who is worthy of reverence in my eyes.
chunks.notes: 'Fulano' is a placeholder meaning 'so-and-so' or 'such-and-such person'—the actual recipient's name would be filled in.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fulanus cupiens me sic beatum exitum instat Felicissimi Opilionis Silvestri summi mortuus aliquibus, sed mihi nec emortuus vivus immo mihi vividus es mortuus sed vivus in me. Hujus laberinthi iter currentes, aut qua insistendo vitare voraginem edoce, aut quo salubres sublati adtingere possint delicias. Quae sentio in hoc cernes; sed sic ego quae et tu comparantur. Cantabres ut pollini. En quae ego tu quae foeliciter redde. Amen. Explicit epistola.
chunks.english: So-and-so, desiring me thus to have a blessed end, urges me like the most blessed shepherd Sylvester, though dead to some, yet not truly dead but alive—indeed you are living though dead, yet alive in me. As we run through the path of this labyrinth, teach me either how to avoid the abyss by careful treading, or how those lifted up in health might reach its delights. What I feel in this matter you will discern, but what I perceive and what you perceive are compared alike. They measure out like flour for weighing. Behold, what I give to you, return happily. Amen. Here ends the letter.
chunks.notes: This cryptic opening establishes a spiritual relationship between author and recipient, using the metaphor of life-in-death common to Christian mystical writing.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: INCIPIT ARGUMENTUM.
chunks.english: Here begins the argument.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non chaos explicant sophistae, bombinare rerum exequar assiduum orbi. Non ocia hoc calamo concussa pistico, nec osilla pinsabunt. Fessus jam hinc perpendiculum ponam. Cujus vocem non poterit compescere aciare, nec Attica Rema inrumine E. A. J. P. Explicit Argumentum.
chunks.english: Sophists do not explain chaos; I shall pursue the persistent droning of affairs throughout the world. No leisure pursuits shake this pen into mystic obscurity, nor will idle trifles grind it down. Already wearied, I shall now set forth my measuring standard. Its voice cannot be restrained by sharpness, nor can Attic Rome silence it with eloquence. Here ends the argument.
chunks.notes: The author positions his work as cutting through rhetorical sophistication to deliver harsh truths. The cryptic letters E.A.J.P. likely represent a motto or dedication now obscure.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Polypticum Proper: On Worldly Ambition
chunks.latin: INCIPIT POLYPTICUM QUOD APPELLATUR PERPENDICULUM.
chunks.english: Here begins the Polypticum, which is called the Perpendiculum.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fragilis pompa hujus aevi languor est et quaeritur, pestis est et omnimodis ambitur. Quae arridet in accessu, cum serpit delectat, in cujus adolescentia est ingens labor, cum intumescit prosternit, et cum recesserit blasphematur, quia solet clientes suos reddere ignobiles, spernentes autem se reddere gloriosos. Non haec mirum quia cum hactenus extiterit ruina pene omnibus sese appetentibus, nec arrogantes deficiunt adhuc, nec acerbitas se elevandi moderatur. Fallentes se quidem praestantiores veteribus, quos provida censura accusat segniores. Horum igitur fasces si enucleati gradatim initientur, dignum judico praenotandos quos rigentes serto exhibent remiditos. Num Chronici fraudant hoc, qui praesertim summum vae, et ipsorum omne concrepant ohe? Haud perstrepunt agmen eorum, qui gradiendo perplexum collem induxerunt sequentes perrecta rubrica quamvis pingant inextricabilem tesseram, qui durantes nec superstites vixere, nec miselli jocundantur. Sed nec autumo sincerum nunc privatim adnotare facies gestorum, nec eos quoniam pupilli musta aetate vident nunc etiam haec clara et hauserant frequenter recitata a suis patribus, et restringunt certius inculcata. Nunc jam in promptu est censere membra praelibatae nequitiae secundum quod cura perlambens repraesentaverit ipocondria stilo.
chunks.english: The fragile pomp of this age is weakness, and yet it is sought after; it is a plague, and yet it is pursued in every way. It smiles upon approach, it delights as it creeps along, in its youthful vigor there is immense toil, when it swells up it casts down, and when it departs it is cursed—because it tends to make its clients ignoble, while making those who despise it glorious. This is no wonder, since up to now it has been the ruin of nearly all who seek it out, yet the arrogant do not diminish, nor does the bitterness of self-advancement grow moderate. They deceive themselves, thinking themselves superior to the ancients, whom prudent judgment accuses of being more sluggish. Therefore, if the bundles of authority belonging to these men are carefully examined step by step, I judge it worthy to mark out in advance those whom they display adorned with a stiff garland. Do not the chronicles expose this, which particularly proclaim the uttermost woe and cry out against all their excesses? The throng does not loudly proclaim those who, by climbing the tangled hill, led their followers according to the direct rubric, though they paint an inextricable pattern—men who, though enduring, neither lived as survivors nor rejoiced as wretches. But I do not think it genuine to record privately the deeds accomplished, nor to record them because their pupils in their youthful age now see these things clearly and have drunk in what was frequently recited by their fathers, and they retain more certainly what has been impressed upon them. Now it is ready at hand to examine the members of this aforementioned wickedness, according to what the care licking over the matter has represented with a melancholic pen.
chunks.notes: This dense opening passage establishes the work's central theme: the corrupting nature of worldly ambition and political power. The author promises to dissect the mechanisms of political corruption systematically.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tale quod resolvimus discrimine trino scanditur fastigium, quidam adjiciunt assem aut cum praeses nominatim signat quem claris nutibus vel cum eadem vox omnium calat. Vel cum eadem unum, aut cum parentis valido jure fas est perfrui throno. Improbus aut labor et cum vesana triumphis praelia conveniunt propere trucibus alteri insurgere quem jam cantaverat praeco prophanis non conjurati manibus execrabiles favent. Quid? Exinanitur aemulus penitus, cujus beneficio ditantur primi velites nec poscunt intemerabile foedus in quo herilis somnus capiat dulcedinem. Sed juvat interim jocundius traducere remissum tempus et placito potiri adeptis ut ambo principes terreantur per adversas lites nec audeant potentes exippitare. Contra milites vel negare quidquam restat ut vigeant principes tantum miserabili nomine ut potius nec compotes sint immo privatae rei compotes. Tum sedulo ruminant et secreta cura rependere sociis has strophas. Quapropter nec taedet clanculo frequentare sese divis ramis per cautos reveredarios qui nec nunciasse frivole piis solertes per quod milites prima instigentur indagine fulti. Externos etiam duces et conniventia rogant et xeniis cumulant et propere macta deponunt. Cur pollicetur extimis fortuna potentum necdum extorta quam ii sequestres gestiunt praelibare vel strictim, ut auxilia eorum ferveant edomare superbos secum, vel si desides torpeant tunc saevire per sua arma denegent subsidia oppressis nam ipsi saevient.
chunks.english: This matter which we are unraveling climbs to its summit by a threefold distinction. Some add their support either when a leader designates someone by name with clear nods, or when the same voice calls out on behalf of all, or when the same voice calls out for one person, or when by a parent's strong right it is lawful to enjoy the throne. But wicked toil comes about when insane battles meet triumphs, and they rush to rise up against another whom the herald had already proclaimed, and those with unhallowed hands abominably favor him, though they have not sworn allegiance. What happens? The rival is thoroughly emptied out, by whose favor the first skirmishers are enriched, and they do not seek an inviolable covenant in which their master's sleep might find sweetness. But meanwhile it pleases them to pass the time more pleasantly in relaxation and to take possession of what they have agreeably obtained, so that both princes may be terrified by opposing lawsuits and the powerful may not dare to stamp their feet in rage. On the other side, nothing remains for the soldiers except to deny anything, so that the princes may flourish in name only, miserably, so that they are not truly in control but rather masters of private affairs only. Then they diligently ruminate and with secret care repay these tricks to their allies. For this reason they do not grow weary of secretly meeting one another in separate branches through cautious messengers, who cleverly have not announced things frivolously to the loyal ones, through which means the soldiers are first instigated, supported by investigation. They also ask foreign leaders for connivance, and heap up gifts upon them, and quickly set down sacrificial offerings. Why does fortune promise to outsiders the power of the mighty, not yet wrested away, which these intermediaries eagerly desire to taste, even briefly—so that their aid may burn to subdue the proud along with them, or if they grow idle and sluggish, then to rage through their own weapons and deny aid to the oppressed, for they themselves will rage.
chunks.notes: This section describes the complex political maneuvering by which nobles and military leaders manipulate weak rulers, cultivate foreign alliances, and play multiple sides against each other for personal gain.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Specimen propterea restat doli quo affecti tribuni eliduntur in imo, cum horum milites captant blandas regias auras; et dictati muniatores redeunt inde ad propria onustis humeris nec ferent duces dominos ipsorum primos arbitros etiam in largiendo beneficia sese praeponent si sint arcta an larga. Et foenerant immunes a proprio, et vehunt sibi fidos detrimento alterius. Qui praeferunt per quos sumunt, sed non a quibus. Si fors separat hos, ipsi ministrabunt donec discant ab alterutro perlata in alterutrum, quae melius silerent. Quos statuunt quidem explorare, et instruere se se. E quibus spargunt per singulas domos velut semina, unde saepe metunt nugas, et saepe praesciscunt expedientia. Hinc milites diluunt primam fidem dominis, firmant servare secundam principibus, quae jam deerit. Tunc notant se infamia, supplantant dominos, nec respuunt denegare supremum. Sic illecti foeda cupidine, quandoque sperant moestis praestare levia si fors differt quicquam periculi utcumque ingerit ex proprio et communando prosternunt. Igitur Principes addunt munire scabrosa et cuncta radosa per exesum ut nil queat quis traducere sibi ignotum. Et cui extant praevalida munimina aut premunt obsidibus, aut nituntur eximere. In quorum fide, si forte credas committere tuum fundum, ut fervent tibi, aut si cures imponere ullum titulum tum metiri propriis haeredibus elogium quidquid utile est cunctis sibi vindicant, neque ponunt dispergere saltem partem in his, quae olim paraverant. Quis struunt arces concilii, ut ne qua incursio sit, quae terreat ipsos, sed tranquille valeant mihi reddere meditata cuilibet. In qua statuunt humatia et componunt tristega. At postquam solide praefixerint labentes plantas, tum diponunt digesta jurgia inter animos procerum, et affirmant se esse socios utrorum adversantium, et plagiatores accusant aemulis aemulos infidos, quod fungantur, ei quoque perversi ut illis. Semotos arguunt tanto crimine, ne pandatur iniqua pharetra, et ne mordeat quemquam indifferenter fore ingratum. Accitos canunt perclues et perdurant dissidium, contestando sacris ne simul redeant cum ipsis ad pristinum, donec versentur in imo et ipsi indices non sequestrantur nitentes has versutas curas et susurret; valebit si haec fuerint deserta infecta. Non lavant insontes, quos nec censura fatigat. Tum Bronchus premitur horrore et vox urgens fulcire edicta politis praestigiis exilii evadit ad aulas stridentibus postibus et lividis pupillis. Hic assertor veri notatur rabido stigmate, ut praeda quantocius discerpenda. Quomodo cum mastrugati Pharti intentant Tragelaphos Scorpus et praevius gradiens captaverit auras lethi, tum se duce contorquens agmina pecudum fugat per aera, cui imputat quisque arcitenens, quod non fuissent omnes prostrati; quem etiam solum imprecans blasphemat, et fatigat compescere. Sic qui valet cognoscere fraudes ducum si protulerit in medium. At consonos mandat caelare coruscis plastis et frequenter advocant martisus et in tritis et emistibus, ut resolvant sese condito dum sunt quibus et horridulis offatim insuper student discludere de cruore aut quae relinquunt quos pessundant. Ministrant his provectum donec. Sed de aliorum jactura. Ex quibus sunt etiam quidam, qui dum aemulant fortunam aliorum sibi indeptam fatigant eos cassare his praestigiis ostendant inlices luctus, et depromunt quod Principes, qui auxerant ipsos fixissent illis aerumnas quos et produnt dignum exludere, et repulsant vindicare haec, et disserunt validum. Illi dum nituntur hoc perpetrare exquirunt modos potius, ut dictant tales amphifari, qui si aspectent jam nactum unde valeant elidere duces, tunc quaerunt inde proventum. Sin ponunt sese cautos quibus et exponunt socios et resolvunt machinas. Et docent primum se vocatos in his ab aliis et mox refugiisse ad eos ut proderent, et exposcunt attribui sibi fidis quae possedere rebelles, ut maneant dotes proditionis; spectant revellere quae tunc cedunt, et prisca dum subpediae fuerint abrasae dimersis procintis et dimersis iis qui vigent solertia; tum et hi luunt detrimenta et tristi successu efficiuntur extorres a commodis nec vegetant ab ullo. Vicissim ejulant; nec deerit titulus facinoris, quo instigetur aerumna, ut caeteris quae heu, proh dolor! rapaces ambrones propalaverunt. Tunc etiam nex atque ruina caeterorum objicitur his, et duces qui fecerunt foeda per omnes accusant hos fomenta scelerum. Pudet se, nec conantur traductos redigere. Sed haec tantum ut possint injicere odia, fama diducit quamdam acerbam avem, quae dum pipliat glicescente genitali calore et blanditur remissa alis subvenit conjux imparis generis non naturae. Quo patrante ales praesenserit se foetam insequitur, gaudens dissolvere dotata viscera sponsi. Sic duces crassantur in exuviis, inhiant ut surgant in alterutrum, ne vana libido impediatur ad omne facinus quod appetit.
chunks.english: Therefore there remains an example of the deceit by which affected tribunes are crushed at the bottom, when their soldiers seek the flattering royal breezes; and the designated fortifiers return from there to their own places with laden shoulders, and they will not tolerate leaders as their masters or primary arbiters, even in bestowing benefits—they will put themselves forward, whether benefits are scarce or abundant. And they lend money free from their own resources, and they transport loyalists to themselves at another's loss. They prefer those through whom they receive benefits, but not those from whom they receive them. If chance separates these people, they themselves will serve until they learn from one another things conveyed between them that would better be kept silent. Indeed, they determine to spy on these people and to instruct themselves. From among them they scatter throughout individual households like seeds, from which they often reap trifles and often learn in advance what is expedient. From this point the soldiers dissolve their first loyalty to their lords and firmly resolve to keep their second loyalty to princes—which loyalty will already fail. Then they mark themselves with infamy, they undermine their lords, and they do not refuse to deny the highest authority. Thus enticed by foul desire, they sometimes hope to provide light relief to the sorrowful if chance brings any danger—however much they heap up from their own resources, they overthrow by sharing. Therefore princes additionally fortify rough places and all scraped-away areas through erosion, so that no one can convey anything unknown to themselves. And those for whom very strong fortifications exist, they either oppress with hostages or strive to remove. In whose trust, if perhaps you believe you should commit your estate so that they may be zealous for you, or if you should care to impose any title, then to measure out the inscription for your own heirs—whatever is useful they claim entirely for themselves, and they do not undertake to distribute even a part among those things which they once prepared. They build strongholds of counsel so that there may be no raid that terrifies them, but they may calmly be able to render to anyone what they have meditated upon. In this they establish foundations and construct three-storied buildings. But after they have firmly fixed their slipping footholds, then they arrange quarrels digested among the minds of nobles, and they affirm that they are allies of both opposing sides, and these kidnappers accuse rivals to rivals as unfaithful, because they act—and they themselves are as perverse as those others. They accuse those set apart with such great crime, lest the wicked quiver be revealed and lest it bite anyone that he will be ungrateful indifferently. They sing of the summoned as very famous and they prolong the dissension, calling upon sacred things that they not return together with them to the former state, until they are turned to the lowest point and the judges themselves, striving, are not separated from these crafty concerns and whisperings. It will prevail if these things have been abandoned unaccomplished. They do not cleanse the innocent, whom judgment does not weary. Then the throat is pressed with horror, and the voice urgently supporting the edicts escapes to the courts with polished deceits of exile, with doors squealing and pupils livid. This champion of truth is marked with a rabid stigma, as prey to be quickly torn apart. Just as when the fur-clad Parthians aim their goat-stags, and Scorpus, going ahead, has caught the breezes of death, then, with himself as leader, turning about, he drives herds of cattle through the air—to him each archer attributes the fact that not all were laid low. Cursing him alone, they blaspheme and weary themselves trying to restrain him. Thus whoever is able to recognize the frauds of leaders, if he brings them into the open—but he commands his allies to be hidden in gleaming moldings, and frequently they call upon warriors both in beaten paths and in purchases, so that they may release themselves, hidden while they are—and to those with horrible morsels they eagerly study to separate them from blood, or whatever they leave behind for those they destroy. They provide advancement for these until— but from the loss of others. Among whom there are also certain ones who, while they envy the fortune of others obtained for themselves, wear them down to nullify them with these deceits—they display enticing sorrows, and they bring forth that the princes who had helped them had inflicted hardships upon them—and they betray them as worthy of being mocked, and they reject vindicating these things, and they argue forcefully. Those men, while they strive to accomplish this, rather seek ways to speak ambiguously about such matters—those who, if they perceive that they have already obtained means by which they can crush leaders, then seek profit from that source. But if they make themselves cautious, to whom they also expose their allies and dissolve their schemes. And they teach first that they were called into these matters by others and soon fled to them in order to betray, and they demand that what the rebels possessed be attributed to themselves as loyal ones, so that the gifts of betrayal may remain. They look to tear away what then yields, and the ancient things—when the auxiliaries have been scraped away by diverse battle lines and by diverse men who flourish in skill—then these also pay penalties, and with sad outcome they are made exiles from advantages and are not refreshed by anyone. In turn they lament; nor will there be lacking a title of crime by which misery is instigated, as with the others which—alas, woe is me!—rapacious gluttons have made public. Then also the death and ruin of others is cast at these men, and the leaders who have done foul deeds through all accuse these men as kindling of crimes. They are ashamed, and they do not try to bring back those who have been led astray. But these things only so that they can cast in hatreds. Rumor spreads about a certain bitter bird, which, while it chirps with swelling genital heat and caresses with wings relaxed, a mate of different kind, not nature, comes to help. When the bird has accomplished this and has sensed itself pregnant, it pursues joyfully to tear apart the endowed entrails of its spouse. Thus leaders grow fat on spoils; they gape to rise up against one another, lest empty lust be impeded from any crime it desires.
chunks.notes: This extended passage provides detailed analysis of how military officers and nobles systematically betray their lords, cultivate networks of spies, manipulate information, play both sides of conflicts, and ultimately create conditions for their own advancement through treachery. The bird metaphor illustrates how allies turn on each other after achieving their immediate goals.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum impacati custodes trahunt diversa exosi inter se, nec bini sentiunt unum, nec singuli possunt resistere vittatim procedunt quocumque tantum est expectare quae actiat urna. Pudet quemquam vibrare ipsum spiritum per superas auras, quaeque manent ingrata cuique dum tenentur auferenda felicior jam proditus, qui immunis extasset. Hinc alii exules repudiant basia nec verentur frustrari natos. Et alii quidem perstringunt lurida viscera paschemate. Et sunt inertes spectantes nodum pyramidis et speleon yconis vel lacunae. Et restant quibus fraudatur aspectus tyrannis vel quos praecisum nomen parentis, videtur servare impubes. Aliis adimitur pars quae experiens bajulat quatuor sensus et revulsa compescit alium. Nec sic odia premunt sopita, sed persequuntur quidquid restat de germine, sed furibunda cupido dum fruitur sontibus, ne quae donat excipiendum nefas effraena saevit in abruptum per ipsos probos. Et postquam insignes diruti compendio fraenant hiare eorum derisores implicantur taediis. Et qui quondam abdicaverunt ipsos irrita fide tum quaerunt seductas dextras jam nullis praediis vel facultatibus dotandas, quas jucundae prius committerent praedictis dominis si servassent prima foedera. Et si origo protulerit hos eugenes niortii continui pareant nequitiae. Post cogunt se ire naufragos abiis pedibus et causantur dediticii, ut vel inviti suppleant quod spoponderant foenerati munerati.
chunks.english: While unpeaceful guardians drag different things, hating one another—neither do two feel as one, nor can individuals resist—they proceed with headbands wherever it is only necessary to wait for what the urn will determine. Anyone is ashamed to breathe his very spirit through the upper air, and whatever things remain ungrateful to each person while they are held must be taken away—more blessed now is he who has been betrayed, who would have been immune. From this point some exiles reject kisses and do not fear to deprive their children. And others indeed pierce pallid entrails with the paschal sacrifice. And there are idle ones watching the knot of the pyramid and the cave of the icon or the pit. And there remain those from whom the sight of tyrants is defrauded, or those whose father's name has been cut off—the youth seems to preserve it. From others is taken away the part which, experiencing, carries four senses, and, torn away, restrains another. Nor do hatreds thus suppress things lulled to sleep, but they pursue whatever remains of the offspring. But raging desire, while it enjoys the guilty—lest it grant anything to be received as wickedness—rages unbridled into the precipice even through upright men themselves. And after distinguished men have been torn down, their mockers, who restrain themselves from gaping at the profit, are entangled in troubles. And those who once renounced them with empty faith then seek seduced right hands, now to be endowed with no estates or resources—hands which they would earlier have pleasantly committed to the aforementioned lords if they had kept the first covenants. And if origin has brought forth these men as well-born, continuous murders obey wickedness. Afterward they compel themselves to go as shipwrecked men with fir-tree feet, and they plead as surrendered ones, so that even unwillingly they may fulfill what they had promised as moneylenders when rewarded.
chunks.notes: This passage describes the devastating consequences of political instability: exiles, executions, mutilations, broken families, and the ultimate collapse of loyalty structures. Former allies seek reconciliation only after losing everything.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Arbitri qui deligantur? quos nec juvat referre spectata, nec juvat spectare inserta vetustis tomis. Intentant haeriles nutus inductos et sic concinnant jura infecta numismate, ut exequata lanx justitiae neminem trutinet facete. Nec omnibus indicetur diligant, indemnis exiet ipse, et quamvis excuset quem exodiunt, vel tenentem fortunam succumbet aut fractus vi aut abactus insidiis. Nec censura durans in idem saepe respondet una sententia, quae nunc arguit, mox iterum inflat laudibus, et rursus proclamat eadem prophana insuper et deteriora. Ast conditor arduae arcis quin laureatus solio nixus elevatur opimo, et foetatur eludere residuam cohortem rudibus syllogismis, jubilat si quos eximet summa dies dum intret caducarius et viduis, quae superstant ab ipsis, non datur optio si sic maneant aut quibus praeparent se bivitas ni redimant quorum inepta connubia aut mercantur ab immeritis aut erigunt egentes clientes regis, qui nec dubitant offuscare privignos nec excludere prodigas matres. Et dux interdum sumet insignitas figuris in Lytostratam aulam turpibus usibus. Deinde cogit privatim degere caeteros productos indigenas et jaceat egenos advenas electis aurilibus quia dapsyles sperat eos sibi.
chunks.english: Who are chosen as arbiters? Those whom it does not please to report what has been observed, nor does it please to observe what has been inserted into ancient volumes. They thrust forward hereditary nods that have been contrived, and thus they fashion laws tainted with bribery, so that the balanced scale of justice weighs no one gracefully. Nor do they love to judge for all—he himself will go out unharmed, and although he may excuse the one whom they send forth into exile, even one holding fortune will succumb, either broken by force or driven away by treachery. Nor does the judgment, lasting the same, often respond with one sentence—that which now accuses, soon again puffs up with praises, and again proclaims the same things profane and even worse. But the founder of the lofty citadel, indeed crowned and leaning on his throne, is raised up in abundance, and he is made fertile to mock the remaining cohort with crude syllogisms. He rejoices if the final day delivers some while the heir enters. And to the widows who survive from them, no choice is given whether they remain thus or to whom they should prepare themselves at the crossroads, unless they redeem those whose unsuitable marriages are either purchased from the undeserving, or they raise up needy clients of the king, who do not hesitate to overshadow stepsons or to exclude wasteful mothers. And the leader sometimes takes women marked with figures into the stone-paved hall for shameful uses. Then he compels the other native people brought forth to live privately, and lets needy foreigners lie with chosen ears, because he hopes generously that they will be his.
chunks.notes: This section critiques corrupt judges who take bribes, reverse their verdicts capriciously, and the ruler who exploits widows and controls marriages for political advantage, treating people as disposable resources.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et si vel cives tantum buccos sequestrante sacra mammona; ut potius celebrent obsequia, et pareant jussis per omne. Nec jactent: haec magnalia fudit nobis genuina prosapia. Quia attoniti plaudunt sed ignari quid rutilet per trabeam, nec docuit praetexta. Obsius fallit hos et licorem quem cauma expulit antro, solent praeferre lapillis indicis et caucasi. Et dum tollunt ciliartica insignia, nec noscunt secunde coaptare. Cumque plebs consulit hos frendentes premunt rudere barbaricas sequelas. Fastidiunt quid reboent leges, et frigore sui barritus perterritant subditos. Et primum gaudent traducere pompam ignotis successibus, ut vero coeperint inspicere mores fortunae licet foris perendinent curas, sed vulnus inspiciatur sub pectore. Sic postquam se dedere quieti altis fulcribus tunc et suscipiunt stephadium sub dio et frequentant clypsidras lucubre et si fallant metiri. Animi fluctuate his dum insuetae rimantur, furtuitae nunc opes, nunc et ruinam expavent degra Principis, ut docuere praecedentes dum crederent tranquillam.
chunks.english: And if only the citizens serve as empty chatterers, with sacred mammon acting as intermediary, so that they might rather celebrate servile duties and obey commands in all things. Nor let them boast: genuine lineage poured forth these great deeds for us. Because they applaud in astonishment but are ignorant of what gleams through the ceremonial robe, nor has the purple-bordered toga taught them. The doorkeeper deceives these people, and they are accustomed to prefer the liquid that heat has driven from the cave to the pebbles of India and the Caucasus. And while they lift up the badges of rank with goat-hair cloth, they do not know how to fit them properly in turn. And when the common people consult these men, gnashing their teeth they press down with braying their barbaric followers. They disdain what the laws echo back, and with the coldness of their trumpeting they terrify their subjects. And first they rejoice to pass along pomp with unknown successions, but truly when they begin to examine the character of fortune—although outwardly they postpone cares for the next day, still the wound is examined under the breast. Thus after they have given themselves to rest on high supports, then they also take up the garland under the open sky and frequently attend water clocks in mourning, to see if they deceive in their measurement. Their spirits fluctuate while they investigate unusual things, now stolen wealth, now also they fear the ruin of the degraded prince, as previous examples taught them while they believed in tranquility.
chunks.notes: The passage describes newly elevated rulers who lack genuine noble lineage and education, who maintain power through intimidation and external display while inwardly anxious about their precarious positions, unable to sleep peacefully despite their luxury.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod nec ducunt veniabile, nec ullus modus excusat praeterire dum quaeque servitus excutitur ab ipsis ut ab immeritis villicis, et neminem excusat inde auctoritas sedis suo respectu, nec doxa fugat contumeliam quos dignitas et eulogiae priscorum caelatae cathalogis retardant. Si origo dedit naevum parant obducere et emicare solum de suscepta dignitate. Hoc stimulant sese. Sed dum conscientia vexat animos degeneris propaginis quos arctius implicat terror revolvunt procacem dominum, et retractant praedecessores et consules et speculantur pervasa quae subegerunt et metuunt exire unde poscunt se sodales minaci perficitur. Dum jam diutina securitas incaute praeparant lapsum, ut fingit didascala series. Nec exlucidant compacta dum ad unum cogant in glomere inductos milites, ut sit ne quis quaerat obsistere ne exulet. Tum una facie prorumpunt incusare decem cui indulgent, et castam fidem servant si licet recedere. Quem etiam si forte opponat lentiginem populo, vel si immurmuret vincilibus compellent compensare suum silere, vel moleste obducunt luci et cogunt experire acerbum iter, et lustrare ignota saecula.
chunks.english: Which they do not consider pardonable, nor does any manner excuse passing over while every servitude is shaken off by them as if by undeserving stewards, and the authority of the seat does not excuse anyone by its own regard, nor does glory drive away the insult from those whom dignity and the praises of ancient men engraved in catalogues hold back. If origin has given a blemish, they prepare to cover it over and to shine forth only from the dignity they have assumed. With this they goad themselves. But while conscience vexes the spirits of degenerate offspring, whom terror more tightly entangles, they revolve in their minds their shameless master, and they reconsider their predecessors and consuls, and they examine the territories they have subdued, and they fear to go out from where they demand that companions be fulfilled by threat. While now long security incautiously prepares a fall, as the teaching series depicts. Nor do they make clear the compacts while they force the led-in soldiers into a single ball, so that no one may seek to resist lest he be exiled. Then with one face they burst forth to accuse the one to whom they grant indulgence, and they keep chaste faith if it is permitted to depart. Whom also, if perchance he opposes freckles to the people, or if he murmurs to prisoners, they will compel him to compensate for his silence, or troublesomely they draw him into the light and force him to experience a bitter journey and to wander through unknown ages.
chunks.notes: This describes rulers of base origin who are hypersensitive about their lack of pedigree, who consolidate military power to prevent dissent, and who eliminate anyone who threatens to expose their inadequacies or past.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc effluunt animis dum compotes caducarii eveniunt prostrati regis. Unde saepe colunt caltudias ostendendo superba, et conducunt se alogiis libare nibaria carcaesia et tribuunt calbas ad invicem et reddunt sabaea semicintia gravata coruscis bullis. Rursus ingeminant se vincire novella fide alternas sibimet mutuo dante prosapia nymphas, et vicissim excipiunt sibi pignora delubro servanda clientela, et vel tali atrabone persistant compacta. Caeteri jurando per Dominum spirituum, et per urnas ejus fidissimorum testium celatas in polito opho. Igitur distinguunt inter se gentem et patriam, et evertunt inopes, et haud odiunt temerarii quaelibet sacra. Fama est cum Autolops coeperit elidere comam hericinae implicat se neci, et tandem et tandem prodere se propria voce. Sic evenit illis qui praesumunt violare sacra. Interea nec cessat punitio, quamvis credantur obliterata, quae dudum tulerant moleste, vel si non fuerint impetrata quae jusserant, opportunitas vindicat in ipsis.
chunks.english: From this point they flow forth in their spirits while, as masters of inheritance, they become prostrate before the king. From which source they often cultivate cleverness by displaying proud things, and they hire themselves out in unreasoning ways to sip at wine-mixing bowls, and they distribute cups to one another and give back Sabaean half-girdles weighted with gleaming bosses. Again they redouble their binding of themselves with new faith, their lineages mutually giving brides to each other in turn, and reciprocally they receive for themselves pledges to be preserved in the shrine through clientage, and they persist in compacts by means of such black bile. The rest swear by the Lord of spirits and by the urns of his most faithful witnesses hidden in the polished receptacle. Therefore they distinguish among themselves race and fatherland, and they overturn the poor, and reckless men do not hate any sacred things whatsoever. The report is that when the antelope begins to crush the mane of the hedgehog, it entangles itself in death and finally, finally betrays itself by its own voice. Thus it happens to those who presume to violate sacred things. Meanwhile punishment does not cease, although they are believed to have been forgotten—those things which they long ago bore with difficulty, or if those things which they commanded were not obtained—opportunity avenges upon them.
chunks.notes: This section describes how these upstart rulers consolidate power through ostentatious display, strategic marriages among themselves, sacred oaths using pagan and Christian elements mixed together, and how they divide society while oppressing the poor—yet divine justice eventually catches up with them despite their attempts to escape consequences.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Insuper tripliciter addunt perficere noxos pro quibus olim finxerant repellere lucem. Quibus praeda est supellex, de qua rursus proclamat curiam, cum nemo sit qui mutiat de curia. Ops considerat longe nec prophetat in his partem secum quos stipant Neotherici buteones, quorum plaudente caterva, mucissonem audies velit si opipare cum ipsis obsoleta veritate proferre. O tempora quae nullum celebrem protrahitis ad judicem et insistant rustici. Recolo Busticeta, sed non propenso consules, quales istos qui sic prospiciunt cuncta librare secundis rebus, dum pertrahunt symphosias musica per urbem modulis inflata, et repercussa, et pulsata proruptis fidibus, inde suppeditat canora vox. Istria dat saltus pincernae invigilant perpetim simul confundere defaecata vina nectare, et ministrant caeliam, et siceram ut fugent parcos, et resolvant temulenta corpora; heu prodentium hinc nefas. Qui perstrepunt quoquo petulcis cachinnis, et mutuo instigant sese jaculando lascivas fabellas. Tunc illecebrae suggerunt stupra et incesta; et servi morigerando dominis pernoctant in his circumquaque bacchantes.
chunks.english: Moreover, threefold they add to accomplishing the destruction of guilty ones for whom they once pretended to repel the light. For whom the plunder is furniture, about which the court again proclaims, when there is no one who mutters about the court. Wealth considers from afar and does not prophesy among these a share with itself—those whom new-fangled buzzards surround, with whose applauding crowd you will hear the driveler, if he wishes to bring forth sumptuously with them, with truth made obsolete. O times, which drag no celebrated man to a judge, yet peasants persist. I recall the funeral pyres, but I do not approve as consuls men such as these who thus look ahead to weigh all things in prosperous circumstances, while they draw out drinking parties with music through the city, swollen with melodies, both echoed and beaten with burst-forth strings—from there a singing voice supplies. Istria provides leaps. Cup-bearers keep vigil perpetually to mix together at once clarified wines with nectar, and they serve beer and cider so that they may put to flight the frugal and dissolve drunken bodies—alas, the wickedness betraying from this source! They make a loud noise everywhere with wanton laughter, and they mutually incite themselves by hurling lascivious tales. Then enticements suggest debaucheries and incests; and slaves, by gratifying their masters, spend the night in these things, reveling all around.
chunks.notes: This passage condemns the dissolute lifestyle of the corrupt nobility: perpetual feasting, drunkenness, sexual immorality, with servants enabling and participating in their masters' vices. The judicial system is paralyzed while peasants seek justice in vain.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed nec poterunt diu perfrui his sine zelo alicujus cum sint pares caeteris ordine, et tanta dignitate praepolleant; nam labor concordiam, dissidium insolentiam parat, et minores semotim accelerant unire se pariter injuria majorum, et causantur deberi sibi plura, quae retinent et castigant exequanda. Hi superant agminibus, et fervet virtus toto robore. Illis viget maturior sensus, et excellunt opibus et firmantur sublimitate moenium. Sed tamen considerant invalidum esse extinguere quod ebullit ni statuant Principem sub quo, sed recusant se statuere. Tandem ineunt consilium proponere sibi stolidum, per quem perficiant quae sunt commoda, qui nec praesumat illos quicquam inquirere vel recusare, quem etiam cogunt haec textare per divina sacra incassum. Nam, cumque fuerit Praelatus, si hebes perstiterit nullum luctum deluet. Quin etiam juvenes potenter jam adjicient derogare priscis. Nec excipiunt dominum nisi segregetur ab illis. Qui facundi contra minantur rudibus infigere deteriora et exercent dominum et requiruntur promissa, ut surgant in adversos, et docent. Quid faciet? poterit ne utrasque vitare ruinas? sed nec ingenia suffragantur inepto. Restat ut effugiat osores, et vacet privatus.
chunks.english: But they will not be able to enjoy these things for long without someone's jealousy, since they are equal to others in rank and excel in such great dignity. For toil prepares concord, dissension prepares insolence, and the lesser nobles separately hasten to unite themselves equally because of the injury of the greater ones, and they argue that more things are owed to them—things which they retain and punish as needing to be carried out. These excel in battle lines, and virtue burns with full strength. For those others, more mature judgment flourishes, and they excel in wealth and are strengthened by the height of their walls. But nevertheless they consider it ineffective to extinguish what boils up unless they establish a prince under whom—but they refuse to establish themselves. Finally they enter into counsel to put forward a stupid man for themselves, through whom they may accomplish what things are advantageous, who may not presume to investigate or refuse them anything, whom they also force to weave these things through divine sacred oaths in vain. For when he has been made prelate, if he has remained dull he will wash away no grief. Indeed, even young men will now powerfully add to derogating from the ancients. Nor do they accept a lord unless he is separated from those men. Those who are eloquent, on the contrary, threaten to inflict worse things on crude men, and they exercise the lord, and the promises are required so that they may rise up against adversaries, and they teach. What will he do? Will he be able to avoid both ruins? But natural talents do not support an incompetent man. It remains that he flee those who hate him and be free as a private citizen.
chunks.notes: This describes internal conflicts among the nobility: lesser nobles unite against greater ones out of resentment, yet both factions are too evenly matched to destroy each other. They attempt to install a puppet ruler who will serve their interests without interfering, but this strategy often backfires when the ruler proves either too weak or too independent.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si vero primum propalaverit se ignavum arte in quo et protinus eluceat prudentia favente diademate: tum perterriti clanculo procurant sopire odia. Et rursus mussant reducere in unum, ut iterum queant retexere haerilem pestem hujusce pacto, quae nec poterit delitescere duci jam clara sui cognitione. Quas nugas parat abolere una cum nugacibus, quem etiam et ipsi adversus se gestiunt excludere ab aucto solio. Quos primum impediunt praecedentia, quae informant ducem. At si arbitrentur haec verenda, et opinentur dumtaxat semet deficere in ipsis, tunc quoque eminus dirigunt gazas conducere sibi heroes. Hinc tenent augenda solatia, quin etiam et parant inde promovere extimum atque robustum ducem, et submittere colla. Tum quidem suffragia et primum per vicarios si non fuerit fa celebrare conventus. Quibus si haec digesta paulisper differantur, deficient imperfecta. An interim cessabit saevire concussus furor praesentis Praesidis, et torpebit depascere fontes? Num insuper enixius aget hostire perpetitores subversis.
chunks.english: But if indeed he first makes public that he is cowardly in skill—in which also prudence immediately shines forth with the crown favoring him—then, terrified, they secretly take care to lull hatreds to sleep. And again they mutter about bringing things back into one, so that again they may be able to unweave the hereditary plague in this way—which will not be able to hide from a leader already clear in his knowledge of himself. These trifles he prepares to abolish along with the triflers, whom they themselves also eagerly desire to exclude from the increased throne against him. Those whom first the preceding things hinder, which inform the leader. But if they judge these things to be feared and think only that they themselves are failing in these matters, then also from a distance they send treasures to hire heroes for themselves. From this source they hold consolations to be increased; indeed, they also prepare from there to promote a foreign and strong leader and to submit their necks. Then indeed they cast votes, and first through deputies if it has not been possible to celebrate an assembly. To whom, if these arranged matters are deferred for a little while, they will fail incomplete. Or meanwhile will the shaken rage of the present president cease to rage, and will he grow sluggish in feeding on the springs? Will he not rather more strenuously act to obtain perpetual inheritors for the overthrown?
chunks.notes: If the puppet ruler shows unexpected competence, the factions briefly reconcile to undermine him, but he now understands their game. They may then seek to import a foreign leader, going through elaborate political maneuvering while the current ruler continues his predations.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et si parent subvenire his quos saepe salutando olim tentabant producere ut primum persenserit hoc sybilum labentis famae, immo declinant et referunt ad aures vigentis ducis quod ipsi ut pestis nimis fuerit exquisita ad ipsos a propriis militibus, quorum animis nullus potuit persuadere et charitate inficiunt relicta quae metus deluerat, et talibus figmentis expectant foenora a latere ejus quamvis infidi proceres; dudum praemiserint his praerogativas quae sunt mercati degenerando quibus sed pro viduis dux pauca porrigit et modestus arguit, si haec maturius panderentur nobis vera aperte dum caeca latenter juvaret. Quorum et invalidos despectat artus cordaque rabida subsannat. Externi sunt etiam Principes, qui cum hauserint lamenta alterius militum perlatores illico deposcunt seductos firmare nequando redeant ad gratiam prisci ducis quibus et mox pari ratione confirmant subvenire.
chunks.english: And if they prepare to help those whom, by often greeting them, they once tried to advance—as soon as he has perceived this hissing of slipping reputation—on the contrary, they turn aside and report to the ears of the flourishing leader that, as a plague, it has been too much sought after against themselves by their own soldiers, to whose minds no one was able to persuade; and they taint with charity what fear had washed away, and with such fabrications they expect returns from his side, although unfaithful nobles. Long ago they sent ahead to these men the prerogatives which they have purchased by degenerating. To whom, however, on behalf of widows the leader extends few things and modestly argues: If these things had been revealed to us more maturely, truly openly, while it would have helped blindly and secretly. And he looks down on their weakened limbs and mocks their rabid hearts. There are also foreign princes who, when they have drunk in the laments of another's soldiers as messengers, immediately demand that the seduced ones be confirmed lest they ever return to the favor of the former leader—to whom also soon by equal reasoning they confirm they will bring aid.
chunks.notes: This describes how the disloyal nobles, when their plotting becomes known, immediately shift to the winning side and denounce their former co-conspirators. Foreign princes exploit internal dissension by offering to support rebels, preventing reconciliation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad quorum Regem tunc caute et citissime dirigunt legatos pandere quae firmaverant milites, sed non quae ipsi ut praestet lepida dona, et cassentur quae jam sunt firmata, et saeviat in suos, quia nec valebunt sustinere requiem in ipsis. Quos etsi procuret cumulare, valebit concitare hostes ad exitium eorum, dum credantur solari. Tunc nectunt foedera prolato talione si expediat. Sunt iterum, qui rogantur invadere alterius Principatus rugiunt et fremunt, et jactant rigida, et latenter accelerant incursare armis incintum ducem. Sed postquam subierint ultra ejus margines stipatum cernant cum, si jam radiante caterva subsistunt et frigescunt animis, et suffugia captant: referre inde pedem. At licet conspiciant suum agmen extare. Si dux eminus procuret indicere illis tranquillos angelos gravatos xeniis inclusi, disputant, et querelantur se seductos, et declamant consules qui ipsos conduxerant sibi perfidos, et innectunt fraudes, quos et deserunt et distinguunt loetho, et tunc pertentant incolumes reducere gressum, et perferre adepta privato solo.
chunks.english: To whose king they then cautiously and very swiftly send legates to reveal what the soldiers had confirmed, but not what they themselves had—so that he may provide charming gifts, and so that what has already been confirmed may be annulled, and so that he may rage against his own people, because they will not be able to sustain peace among themselves. Even if he should try to heap benefits upon them, he will be able to stir up enemies for their destruction while they are believed to be comforting. Then they weave treaties with retaliation set forth, if it is expedient. There are again those who, when asked to invade another's principality, roar and rage and brandish harsh words, and secretly hasten to attack with arms the unprepared leader. But after they have entered beyond his borders and perceive him fortified, if now with a shining company, they stop and grow cold in spirit and seek refuges to withdraw their foot from there. But although they perceive their own battle line to stand firm—if the leader from a distance takes care to proclaim to them peaceful messengers laden with gifts and enclosed, they dispute and complain that they have been seduced, and they declaim that the consuls who had hired them for themselves are treacherous, and they weave frauds, whom they also desert and mark for death, and then they attempt to bring back their steps unharmed and to carry back what they have obtained to private soil.
chunks.notes: Foreign rulers exploit internal conflicts by encouraging rebellion, then betraying their supposed allies when convenient. Mercenary forces, seeing a prepared defense, immediately abandon their employers and seek to negotiate safe return, blaming those who hired them for the failed invasion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Raro est, sed est qui inductus aggreditur expugnare dicatum ducem nudo pectore, nec desistit, donec adeptus sit orcum vel palmam aut per cladem belli dividat cum altero et contenti partibus coeant in unum foedus securus quis edomare sua. Quid proficient milites, qui hunc provocaverant? si in tali discrimine efflet supremum superstes expectet hos poenae, et si compos evaserit, tum et isdem compellat ad id nec ponit jam extare sibi intemerabile thronum hujusmodi quae fuerant recolens, ni auctoritate priventur. Sunt quibus praesidet Princeps unius Provinciae quos etsi summo tenus laeserit turgescunt siquidem rumina yperifania, et protinus adeunt verendum trino diademate et incitant auferre quibus potitur impar dux. Sin explicent, poterint ne habere recursum ad propria? et quamvis perfecerint, ferent tandem competentia.
chunks.english: It is rare, but there is one who, once led in, undertakes to attack a dedicated leader with bare chest, and does not desist until he has obtained either death or the palm of victory, or through the disaster of war divides with the other, and, content with shares, they come together into one treaty, each secure to subdue his own. What will the soldiers profit who had provoked this man? If in such a crisis he breathes his last, let the survivor await punishment for them; and if he has escaped in control, then he compels them to the same thing, and no longer does there exist for him an inviolable throne—remembering such things as these had been—unless they are deprived of authority. There are those over whom a prince of a single province presides, who, even if he has injured them only superficially, nevertheless swell with the rumination of excessive pride, and immediately they approach the one to be revered with the triple crown and incite him to take away what things the unequal leader possesses. But if they accomplish this, will they be able to have recourse to their own lands? And although they have accomplished it, finally they will bear appropriate consequences.
chunks.notes: Rarely, a true warrior fights to the death or until decisive victory, but even this creates problems for those who instigated the conflict. Provincial nobles who appeal to higher authority (the Pope, implied by 'triple crown') against their local ruler may succeed in the short term but cannot return home and face eventual justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeminens conducet sibi in obsequium diversas acies nationum et diffusa verba quae nesciant vivere parce, sed compilare quaeque potiora, et in sacris addere matronas ut postribulo. Et ut persenserint depopulatam Patriam non traducitur statim, quia invalidum est diu continere phalanges in externis, nec convenit tanto duci remotum a sua legione degere quidem cum ipsis de quorum exili fide oppido veretur. Redit ad proprium solum quod gaudet revisere. Quem si forte proponant deducere qui eum solemne induxerant, eligant lugubre exilium jugi confusione manente ruinae sin potius ignominiosae linquuntur. Interea dux praesentis Patriae inferior milibus vel exulat profunde, vel residens obtrusus moenibus praevalide urbis ejulat. Sed cum noverit discessum hostis optatam diem convocat disclusos quos signaverat clara fides in aciem. Et instruit primipilos, et disponit conferre cuique debita fidis laeta, et sinistris acerba.
chunks.english: The preeminent one will hire for himself in service diverse battle lines of nations and scattered peoples who do not know how to live frugally but to plunder whatever is best, and to add married women to sacred places as if to a brothel. And when they have perceived that the fatherland has been devastated, he is not immediately led across, because it is ineffective to keep phalanxes long in foreign lands, nor is it fitting for so great a leader to remain remote from his own legion, living indeed with those men whose thin faith he greatly fears. He returns to his own soil, which he rejoices to revisit. Whom, if perhaps those who had solemnly led him in propose to lead back down, they choose mournful exile with perpetual confusion of ruin remaining, or rather they are left ignominiously. Meanwhile the leader of the present fatherland, inferior by thousands, either exiles himself deeply or, remaining shut up within the walls of a very strong city, laments. But when he has learned of the departure of the enemy, on the desired day he calls together into battle line those who had been separated, whom clear faith had marked. And he instructs the centurions, and he arranges to confer on each what is owed: joyful things to the faithful, bitter things to the sinister.
chunks.notes: When a superior power intervenes militarily, they hire foreign mercenaries who plunder indiscriminately and desecrate churches. But such forces cannot be maintained indefinitely far from home. Once they withdraw, the original ruler emerges from hiding or exile to reward the loyal and punish traitors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Summe his qui accierant tyrannum per se addicitur mulctatio et poena et inde supplicium. Nec solum dux est exosor, sed et agmina quae pertulerant contumelias infigunt odia; nec tantum, sed et pariter conantur egestare minores ipsorum, donec aetas gradatim demoliatur hoc totum in annis et si forte funus praecidat hos in istis superest, ut nec valeant gratulari labenti aevo nec futuro. Quapropter non deperit vitium. Rursus insurgunt alii et rotantur iisdem. Sed quia sunt qui neque fuerant Cyrrati nec poterant pertingere vel laciniam carbasi prae fulgore sophiae et quibus nihil est si duus deferantur ad urnam sandapila, ut liceat eis perferre indemnem domoictionem refertis manubiis ii stimulant memet sic lacessere erroneae inluderis Prete si tuleris confundere sigillata has seculi si fuerint exercita vel effusa quae promis floccipendenda tunc et natura sterilis linquat sua munera si poteris fingere eumdem statum summati et lixae, quibus prospicere unum haeteris et aridae et Cocyti quid derogas officiis Praesidum?
chunks.english: Especially upon those who had summoned the tyrant for themselves is imposed fine and penalty, and from that, punishment. Nor is the leader alone filled with hatred, but also the battle lines that had endured insults inflict hatreds; and not only that, but equally they attempt to impoverish their inferiors, until age gradually demolishes all this over the years—and if perhaps death cuts short these men in these matters, it remains that they cannot rejoice in declining age nor in the future. For this reason the vice does not perish. Again others rise up and are turned by the same wheel. But because there are those who neither had been tonsured nor were able to reach even the hem of fine linen because of the brightness of wisdom, and to whom it is nothing if they are carried to the urn in a poor man's bier—so that it may be permitted to them to carry through an unharmed home-going with laden spoils—these men goad me to challenge thus: You mock, wandering astray, priest, if you will have borne to confound these sealed things of the age, if what you bring forth as worthless will have been exercised or poured out, then let even sterile nature abandon its gifts if you will be able to fashion the same status for the summit and for the camp-follower—you who are able to look ahead to one thing, both of the desert and of the river of lamentation. Why do you derogate from the duties of presidents?
chunks.notes: The victorious ruler punishes not only the ringleaders but extends vengeance to their families and followers for generations. Yet the cycle continues—new factions arise and repeat the same patterns. At this point, critics challenge the author: you're a cleric with no worldly goods, what right do you have to criticize rulers?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: an vales obfuscare suppetias, quas ipse summe potens legerat ut fulciret labantem orbem. Non confiteris Malachim aut minime plaudis cui concentus virginum dat trophaeum decem millium hostium? non potius assumis hoc quam haecaton prophoneuma occulis gradibus revocaveras cursum nec refers procanum templi cohelectae qui et detersit abominanda? et ut nunc subeant novum testamentum in ordine Helenae filium strenuum strophicis armis qui removens pancratiarii Christicolis firmavit jura officii. O Dux Theodosi magnus tantis triumphis nec duceris ore hujus tu qui respuens haereses fudisti barbara tela et destruens phana sancxisti deleri titulos ne restaret saltem hypogaeum in quo lateret quoddam residuum ex sacro Daemonis, sed cuncta militarent uno auctori. O inducte ne reputas aequivocum nepotem hujus extare sublimem, qui temperans prunas pruna, atque iterum pruinas pruna domavit libidinem et furorem dogmate et fidus charitati, nec extiterat segnis aut cadens tristitiae et subindius triumphaverat supplicans aeterno quam armis.
chunks.english: Or are you able to obscure the aid which the most powerful one himself had chosen so that he might support the falling world? Do you not acknowledge the angels, or do you not applaud at all him to whom the harmony of virgins gives the trophy of ten thousand enemies? Do you not rather take up this matter than—you who had called back the course by hidden steps to the hundred preliminary offerings, and do you not report concerning the forecourt of the temple of the gathered ones, who also wiped away abominations? And so that now they may enter the New Testament—in the order of Helena's vigorous son with warlike arms, who, by removing the all-combatant, established for Christians the rights of duty. O leader Theodosius, great with so many triumphs, you are not led by the mouth of this man—you who, rejecting heresies, scattered barbarian weapons and, destroying temples, sanctioned that titles be destroyed lest there remain even an underground chamber in which some remnant of the demon's sacred rite might hide, but that all things might serve one author. O led one, do you not consider that the namesake grandson of this man exists sublime, who, tempering coals with coal and again frosts with frost, tamed lust and fury with doctrine and, faithful to charity, was neither sluggish nor falling into sadness, and repeatedly triumphed by supplicating the eternal one more than by arms?
chunks.notes: The author responds by invoking Christian history: Do you deny angelic assistance to rulers? Do you not celebrate how Constantine (son of Helena) established Christianity, how Theodosius the Great destroyed paganism completely, how his grandson Theodosius II governed virtuously through wisdom rather than force?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid proficit plebeia manus? nec ipsa subsistet singula exhausta ducibus, quod prima secula signat notamus facile et est hinc apponere curae. Validi armis artubus excreverant primis seminibus Adae et foecunda proles paravit opimas turbas, crescens crebris successibus et raro exexu. Sed Praeses instructus defuit erudire rudes cohortes, unde evitarent discrimen undantis limphae. Ipse Abrahamicus populus plebs Dei est auctus triumphis dum supplex deguit substitiosis, quod quia exorbitatus contempsit, exulat procul a limine et extat inutilis strips. Cujus nec hoc solum est, sed cuique terrigenae restat. Et quod notantur tantum homines quorum mentem quod praecipuum est fatemur esse cum Deo? Sunt etiam exiguae volucres et providae quae vigent solertia et quibus ceditur ferre perenne coelibatum per auras, quae colunt Stratilaten in se, et reddunt debita Stralitati. Quem et ceterae monstrant sospitem si sint alacres. Pro cujus etiam funere si sit, rursus squalent. Sed si discedat a propriis cunctae linquunt serias cum quo externas piget eas nec inquirere sedes.
chunks.english: What does the common multitude accomplish? Nor will it itself subsist, each one exhausted without leaders—which the first ages mark. We note this easily, and it is fitting to apply care from this point. Strong men in arms and limbs had grown from the first seeds of Adam, and fertile offspring prepared rich crowds, growing with frequent successes and rare destruction. But an instructed leader was lacking to train the raw cohorts, so that they might avoid the danger of the flooding water. The very people of Abraham, the people of God, was increased with triumphs while it lived humbly under foundations; because, having gone astray, it despised this, it is exiled far from the threshold and exists as a useless lineage. And this is not only its case, but it remains for every earth-born. And why are only those men marked whose mind—which is the chief thing—we confess is with God? There are also tiny birds, and provident ones, which flourish with skill and to whom it is granted to bear perpetual celibacy through the air, which worship the general within themselves and render what is owed to generalship. Whom the others also show to be safe if they are vigorous. On account of whose death also, if it should occur, they again become squalid. But if he departs from his own, all abandon serious pursuits, with whom it displeases them neither to seek out foreign dwelling places.
chunks.notes: The author now shifts to providential history: Common people need leaders, as seen from Adam onward. Israel prospered under good leaders and suffered without them. This principle applies universally. Even bees (the 'tiny birds' who remain 'celibate') show the necessity of leadership—when their leader dies, the hive falls into disorder until a new one emerges.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nempe popellus proficit sub sedulo et Cato duce sine quo instructa regio exinanitur. Desine nunc coeptos et utere nostris sensibus. Juvat memet nunc fabre suggillare eos pequatos. O quos sat est distrahere effusa vestris emicadiis. Quorsum! ut possitis fore vel symbolones magnatum. In quorum talione. Dumtaxat nugae redduntur delenefici videtur pleumon dum angitur tuccetis non durat exprimere solum panegyrica. Elluones sicut clave suspendat vos ab uno edulio, mox cantilando curatis procul insectari vel lentaculum et exterius libare tementum et inde gyrando parare ferculum, vel si hoc deficiat obolam insanea si praeda sit remota abscessu vel si privetur auctoritate. Non differtur crapula utcumque, dum praegravidus aqualiculus repellat acerbum fascem. Fraternitas quae in vobis fatetur a vobis sola arvalis est quibus jam staret sarchiam ut ne ferendis; sed vergitur quod pectore vulnus consumens haec in tenui liquore; non desinit hin foetare atrum bilem.
chunks.english: Indeed, the little people profit under a diligent leader, and without a Cato as guide the well-ordered region is emptied out. Stop now what you have begun and make use of our senses. It pleases me now to skillfully mock those who have been marked as sinful. O you whom it is enough to tear apart with things poured out by your glitterings! To what end? So that you might be able to be even sharers of great men? In whose retaliation—insofar as trifles are rendered—the charm-maker seems to be the lung while he is constricted. You hasten; it does not last to express only panegyrics. Gluttons—as if a club suspends you from a single meal—soon by singing you take care to pursue far off even a light breakfast, and outwardly to pour libations of intoxicating drink, and from that, by circling, to prepare a dish; or if this is lacking, a penny—you rage if prey is removed by absence or if authority is deprived. Drunkenness is not deferred in any case, while the very pregnant little water-carrier repels the bitter burden. The brotherhood which confesses among you is from you alone agricultural—for whom it would now stand to weed so as not to be borne; but it tends toward what the wound in the breast, consuming these things in thin liquid, does not cease from this point to bring forth black bile.
chunks.notes: The author continues addressing his critics directly: You're just parasites hoping to share in the wealth of the powerful! You rush from one meal to the next, always drinking, making yourselves sick with gluttony. Your supposed brotherhood is shallow, masking inner corruption and bitterness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: O prius caperent vos tantum subgrundaria quam nunc polynctos sarcophaga. Et si Pegaso optat vos protrahi in istis usque ad silicernias non spectat tum capulum sed jucunda ora, neque sublustrat vispillones, nam licet validum esset vobis inficere faciem coeli manalibus non est inficere sensum viri quod bacchantes tristibus rictibus minamini cassare verum ducentes aemula ora ad Valgias et ad levem baubatum? ut cacabant humida aritina. Cum hauserint ignem sic stitiant temulentia in ipsis stultis. Respuo quod nictent taciti quorum et frigultire contemno et garrire nauci pono. Non est mihi diobolaris lingua defluens quaque ad naenias, sed si vestra syngrapha moveant injuriam meo stylo stat ut nostrae paginae ducantur ad sennion. Mea quam nec obelus signabit merebitur ne inhaereat nocuum sigim. Licet nunc etiam sedare rumores quos blaterando dedistis populis se si deinde pululent arphagantibus ullis futilibus tunc urbanum erit tueri in ipsis jamdudum promissa.
chunks.english: O that you had formerly taken only undergarments rather than now the many-folded sarcophagi! And if by Pegasus it is desired that you be dragged in these up to the twilight chambers, he does not then look at the handle but at pleasant faces, nor does he illuminate the undertakers—for although it would be powerful for you to stain the face of heaven with flowing streams, it is not possible to stain the sense of a man. What, reveling with grim grimaces, do you threaten to nullify the truth, leading rival mouths to bandy-legged ones and to the light babbler, as the moist Aretines cackled? When they have drunk fire, thus drunkenness thirsts in those fools themselves. I reject what they wink at in silence—their chattering I despise and their babbling I consider worthless. My tongue is not a two-penny one flowing away toward lullabies, but if your writings stir up injury, it stands with my pen that our pages be led to seriousness. My work, which not even an obelus will mark, will deserve that no harmful mark adhere to it. It is permitted now also to calm the rumors which you gave to the peoples by blathering—but if afterward they sprout for any vain boasters, then it will be urbane to defend among them what was promised long ago.
chunks.notes: The author mocks his critics as corpse-like, their words empty despite their threats. He insists his critique is serious scholarship that will endure, while their rumors and complaints are ephemeral nonsense. He reserves the right to respond more fully if they continue attacking.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praetores nunc acies luscinorum poterunt perspicere sudo ullum flosculum perpendiculi etsi sit? Sed erucae more quaerunt ammordere non quod sit sapor in ore, sed ut pessundent devorando quos ego horticola ostans repagulis curo excludere. Adeant miropolam delegant colliria et comant allucinaria, ut post secernant lucidum et opacum. Non ego passus loetargum praesumo pellere officia praesidum; sed castigo non temeranda. Quia quod jam retuli grave est inepte scandere summum conniti, et subigere captivam patriam et frustrari alterum solio. Pormia et praeda erit hoc, et si deneget quis, potius est ducere pellere primatum quam auferre. An levius a proprio non fert exulat qui limine tractus premitur in ipso ut baro quam qui rusticus alterius inibi condensare primos quos sibi struxerat absque cujus jure ne parum permordeat inde vel qui vadatus numellis et absteniis dum sint cuncta diruta cernit mulcere propriam matronam saviis, et sentiat nepotem vi corrumpere himenaeum suae familiae, quin etiam et deludere procos earum seducta spe clientelae.
chunks.english: Will the magistrates now be able to perceive with keen vision any little flower of the measuring line, even if it exists? But in the manner of caterpillars they seek to gnaw at it, not because there is taste in the mouth but so that they may destroy by devouring—whom I, as gardener, standing at the barriers, take care to exclude. Let them approach the perfume-seller, let them assign eye-salves and let them treat their hallucinations, so that afterward they may distinguish the bright and the dark. I, suffering from lethargy, do not presume to drive away the duties of presidents, but I correct things not to be treated rashly. Because what I have already reported is serious: to climb foolishly to the summit, to strive, and to subjugate a captive fatherland and to deprive another of his throne. This will be monstrous and prey, and if anyone denies it, it is rather better to lead and to drive out the chief than to take away. Or does he bear it more lightly, who is dragged from his own threshold and pressed down in exile on it like a baron, than he who, as a rustic of another, in that place must witness first ones whom he had built up for himself being concentrated, without whose right he is not little bitten from that—or he who, pledged with coins and kept fasts, while all things are torn down, sees his own wife soothed with kisses, and perceives his grandson violently corrupting the marriage of his household, and indeed also mocking their suitors with the hope of clientage having been seduced?
chunks.notes: The author defends his measuring standard (perpendiculum) as necessary moral correction. His critics are like caterpillars trying to destroy what they cannot understand; they need spiritual healing. He insists he's not attacking legitimate authority, only correcting abuses. The specific evil he condemns: unjust usurpation that leaves the rightful ruler either exiled or humiliatingly powerless in his own territory, watching his family dishonored and his property seized.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Difficile est alicui invadere regnum si haec arguat quem delectat pilemum quid hinc est oriendum? forte interea oppetet et decedet barathro; sed qui inhiat descriptos moerores parare caeteris aut ignorat avernum, aut impavidus expectat. Quem ipse sator omnium volvet in sententiam, ut ignobilis discedat ab hoc aevo et subeat supplicium futuri. Igitur quem Omnipotens vult ostentare dignum suis prognosticis vel quem una quaerit promovere conventui omnium procerum, et quem juvat ad id decorus patrius amor fas est ascendere vacantem thronum. Sed cum antestat, sed caveat quod populo ne dividiae crescant, sed ejus ascensus removeat omne justitium et si sit. Qui tali sanctione evaserint ad regnum forsitan beando patriam poterunt obtinere felicem supremum diem, caeteri exuti luerunt poenam tyrannidis, quam et tenendo protraxerunt ramenta Pandecten poterit reservare vobis haec in gymnasio; cujus millenas facies et si placet revolvere non opponet obicem meis scriptis, sed ipse succurret testibus at ut stylus redeat ad ipsos quos opeligna cohors curastis praenotare in faciem ante proles Ysai perspiraverat mutare pedum in scipione, usque dum quaesita oratione videntis sit prolata virtute mystici crysmatis in vertice?
chunks.english: It is difficult for anyone to invade a kingdom if he accuses him whom the cap delights—what is to arise from this? Perhaps meanwhile he will meet and depart to the abyss; but he who gapes to prepare described sorrows for others either ignores hell or fearlessly awaits it. Whom the very sower of all will roll into judgment, so that ignoble he may depart from this age and undergo the punishment of the future. Therefore, the one whom the Almighty wishes to display as worthy of his predictions, or the one whom alone he seeks to promote to the assembly of all nobles, and the one whom honorable paternal love helps to this—it is lawful for him to ascend the vacant throne. But when he stands before it, let him beware that riches not increase for the people, but that his ascent remove all dispensing of justice—if there is any. Those who by such sanction have escaped to the kingdom, perhaps by blessing the fatherland, will be able to obtain a happy final day. The rest, stripped, will pay the penalty of tyranny, which also by holding they have dragged out. The Pandects will be able to preserve these scrolls for you in the school, whose thousand faces, if it pleases you to turn over, will not place an obstacle to my writings, but will itself come to the aid of witnesses. But so that the pen may return to those very ones whom the work-loving cohort took care to mark in the face—before the offspring of Jesse had sweated to change from feet to a staff, until by the prayer sought, the virtue of the mystical anointing might be brought forth on the head by the seer?
chunks.notes: The author warns that usurpers face divine judgment and eternal damnation. Only those whom God approves and whom legitimate succession favors may rightfully ascend the throne—and even they must rule justly, not for personal enrichment. He references his works finding place in libraries alongside legal texts. The reference to Jesse's offspring is to David, who was anointed by Samuel after being chosen by God rather than claiming power by force.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Recolo Exechiam et Josiam a genitali sanguine Regum prodiisse Reges favente populo. An non fuerat ejus genitor sopitus in sede regni, cujus genetrix Helena? qui dum ageret providentia voluit sibi consedere natum. Et Theodosius ortus est claris militibus Hispaniae. Auxilio primi a barbaris qui reddidit tutum orbem; cujus quoque indiciis primum intenderat se lavari ad summa. Inquam. Cernens se in oromate indutum clamyde imperii, et ferentem stegma impositum. Qua indagine potuit noscere eumdem ante invisum sibi nullo indice cum quandoque conspiceret ejus vultum. Qui mox promotus ab Imperatore Gratiano efficitur consors, quod manebat ipsa visio urgentibus bellis, quae is sedavit interea. Et inde decuit suum aequivocum nepotem aequipedantem meritis tranquille subsedere thronum patris et avi. Si in hac norma alicui regale solium hunc jam non tenebit culpa et si sedent si censeat semet sufficere ad nomen tantae majestatis pro quo sed minime jubilet adhuc se manere securum. Sola auspicia licet existant digna, tamen non supplent praebere felicem, sed quaeruntur incrementa.
chunks.english: I recall that Hezekiah and Josiah came forth as kings from the royal bloodline, with the people favoring them. Was not his father laid to rest in the royal seat, whose mother was Helena? Who, while providence was acting, wished his son to sit beside him. And Theodosius was born of renowned soldiers of Spain, with the help of the first one from the barbarians who rendered the world safe.
