By whose signs also he first intended to bathe himself for the heights. I say, seeing himself dressed in the ornament with the cloak of empire and bearing the crown placed upon him. By what investigation was he able to recognize the same one, previously unseen by him with no indicator, when at some point he beheld his face? Who, soon promoted by the Emperor Gratian, is made partner—which vision remained with urgent wars, which he calmed in the meantime. And from that point it was fitting that his namesake grandson, walking equal in merits, calmly took his seat on the throne of father and grandfather. If in this pattern the royal throne comes to anyone, guilt will not hold this one now. And if they sit, if he judges himself sufficient for the name of such great majesty—on account of which, however, let him by no means rejoice that he remains secure. Auspices alone, although they exist as worthy, nevertheless do not suffice to provide happiness, but increases are sought.
chunks.notes: The author provides positive examples of legitimate succession: Hezekiah and Josiah succeeded righteously in biblical history. Constantine (whose mother was Helena) was providentially chosen. Theodosius the Great, though born a Spanish soldier not of imperial family, was divinely indicated through a vision and legitimately elevated by Emperor Gratian. His grandson inherited justly. But even legitimate rulers must not be complacent—good beginnings require continued virtuous growth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde libet mihi nunc ponere pasta dogmata λογω ΚυνΝου Saul levatus est de parva traduce ut tueretur Israel. Hinc Palaestinos trudit in principio sui regni et caeperat per gyrum exinanire coeteras perduelles nationes levibus triumphis. Quem ut Pleonexagia coegit inhiare vetitis mox ejus teretes lacerti torpendo cessarant a pugna. Cujus etiam terribola mens non praesumens spectare aciem hostis proprio telo impegit semet in etneas poenas. Et Eudoxius princeps tulerat regem Manassen de redivivo sanguine; postquam enim Deus quindecim annos Ezechiam auxit ad vitam postea iste natus est Manasses. Cui succedens in throno censuit interim extingui quae genitor flagrare et quae ipse prodit subcisiva is sancivit supplicanda impugnans Theusebiam et reducens orgia Daemonis et phana.
chunks.english: From this source it pleases me now to set forth teachings fed to the Cynic reasoning. Saul was raised up from a small lineage so that he might protect Israel. From this point he drove back the Philistines at the beginning of his reign and had begun around the circuit to empty out the other enemy nations with easy triumphs. When greed compelled him to gape at forbidden things, soon his rounded arms, growing sluggish, had ceased from battle. His also terrifying mind, not presuming to look upon the battle line of the enemy, drove himself with his own weapon into Etna's punishments. And the glorious prince bore King Manasseh from revived blood; for after God added fifteen years to Hezekiah's life, afterward this one, Manasseh, was born. Who, succeeding to the throne, decided meanwhile to extinguish what his father had caused to burn—and what he himself brought forth as cut under, this one sanctioned to be supplicated, attacking piety and bringing back the orgies of the demon and temples.
chunks.notes: The author shifts to biblical negative examples: Saul began well but fell into disobedience and died shamefully. Manasseh, though son of the righteous Hezekiah, restored idolatry and reversed his father's reforms. Even legitimate succession provides no guarantee of good rule.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Licinius quippe donec tendit collega in agmine militum scitalis adusque obtinuit illicere mentes eorum ut urgerent cum favoribus inepte potiri calculo imperii sub quo et concinant se impune silere. Quod illuserat sedulum Augustum ut et ipse fulciret quem etiam applicuit in sodem haud deflans jugare suam germanam obauxerat se tunc tantisper rudi toga spirans orthodoxis secunda et ruens demolatria. Tum anxerat Delphicos et pressit tegularia, et prorsus abjurabat acceptatores methanicae artis, et instauraverat asseclas, et spiculatores quos prisci arcontes adlexerant sibi ut attaminarent cimilia cum ipsis idoneis. Anna non jam caluisset inchoata. Sed cujus praesentia ut fuerat auctorata per climata desipuit, quin appendit tunc recusare alme mulgata nuper cruentis antitethis. Jam si quis confiteretur Jesum primum destinabat eum sectoribus, et inde lanystis. Et mox donavit omnes bacare Bromio et simul Chinthio, et complicibus eorum cubo tenus.
chunks.english: Indeed, Licinius, while he served as colleague in the battle line of soldiers, even by lots obtained to entice their minds so that they might urge him with favors to foolishly obtain a share of the empire—under which they might also sing together that they were unpunished in silence. Which had mocked the diligent Augustus so that he himself also might support him, whom he also joined to the seat, not ceasing to yoke his own sister. He had increased himself then for a time with the rough toga, breathing favor toward the orthodox and rushing into the demolition of idolatry. Then he had strangled the Delphic ones and pressed down the tile-makers, and absolutely he abjured the receivers of vain art, and he had restored the followers and spies whom the ancient rulers had enlisted for themselves so that they might pollute treasuries with those same suitable ones. The year would not now have grown bald, having been begun. But when his presence had been authorized through the regions, he played the fool; indeed, he then added to refuse what had been kindly promulgated recently with bloody oppositions. Now if anyone should confess Jesus, first he destined him to the executioners, and from that to the wool-workers. And soon he granted all to revel for Bacchus and at the same time for Cynthius, and for their accomplices, even to lying down.
chunks.notes: This describes Licinius, co-emperor with Constantine, who initially presented himself as favorable to Christianity to maintain his position, even marrying Constantine's sister. He suppressed pagan oracles and magic arts. But once secure in power in his own territories, he reversed course and launched terrible persecutions against Christians, dedicating everything back to the pagan gods Bacchus and Apollo (Cynthius).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea erexerat tunc romphaeam contra Principem Constantinum, a quo est stipulatus in regno, et adtinxit etiam conglorium ejus cognationis. Sed illico ut haec sunt ei traducta refragans ipsa exemit eum a numine quin et firmavit tersa jura. Digna quidem speties vix pullulat, vel nequando genere. Neque extat etiam ab expectabili indifferenter provenit ad perfectum venit, sed subinde defluit nequam a praecipuo genere. Et inde est crimen ruere ad sceptra ut tonant praetaxata. Sed nec sola aequa dicatio perficiet quemquam magnum, ni honestus praetendat quoque dignitatem imbutis moribus quoad valeat conscendere in aeternum regnum. Et hae filacteriae debentur omnes redimiis Christi. Cuique servare modestum vitae in propria mensura. Ambire extra ponat accidiam, et sit abrogans neque sit congerro cui sat sint beneficia, quae juste liquerant ei antigenae, et quae impendit herilis liberalitas, cogente illibato famulatu, et quae fundit exacta rusticatio de proprio jure, et quae rite depromit injuncta ars.
chunks.english: Moreover, he then raised the sword against Prince Constantine, from whom he had secured his position in the kingdom, and he also stained the kinship relationship of his family. But immediately when these things were reported to him, resisting, she herself removed him from divine power; indeed, she also established purified rights. Indeed, a worthy appearance scarcely sprouts forth, or never by birth. Nor does it exist either from the expected one; indifferently it comes to the perfect, it comes, but immediately it flows down wicked from the chief kind. And from this source is the crime of rushing to scepters, as those things mentioned before resound. But not even right dedication alone will make anyone great, unless honorable he also extends dignity with morals imbued, insofar as he is able to ascend into the eternal kingdom. And these phylacteries are owed to all the crowns of Christ. To each one to preserve a modest life in his own measure. To strive beyond, let him set aside sloth, and let him be one who repeals, and let him not be a comrade to whom the benefits are sufficient that ancestors justly left clear to him, and what master's generosity bestows, with unblemished service compelling, and what collected agriculture pours forth from proper right, and what a skill assigned properly brings forth.
chunks.notes: Licinius even turned militarily against Constantine, his benefactor and brother-in-law. Constantine's sister Constantia interceded, but ultimately divine justice removed Licinius. The author draws a moral lesson: Worthy character rarely emerges and rarely endures through generations; people of base character seizing power is the recurring crime. Legal right to rule is insufficient—one must also live virtuously with modest contentment within one's proper station, working diligently, avoiding laziness and greed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et si forte majores sedule delegant officia, suppleat non gestiendo hic pompam, sed perennem. Quae si referant emolumentum, rependat pariter laudes et obsequia aeterno. Et quamvis patiatur deliquium non expers deserat flagitare eumdem nec blasphemat in egestate ignarus qua sententia sit tactus. Num nequeunt succedere inopinatae vices? Nam si Caenodoxus inhiet e gratus auctionari prae se poterit hic elisus erinis, et retrudi crebo. En! Sed quid ego profusus patagine, instar emphatici praeconis enitor explicare digestam linquentis saeculi, cujus morbos nec diserta glossa suffert conjicere? Sed deterius languit quondam dum Clinicus, et depressus coeno congeminans trenos tabebat jam defreta salute; cui etiam protoclectes superastabat demoliri virosis morsibus. Sed respiciens interim qui conlinierat ipsum vola subvenit adtrito, et excussit eum ab ore ejusdem, quem evitans etiam adentavit; et resolidans tunc solutum, et elumbem mundum firmavit stare.
chunks.english: And if perhaps superiors diligently delegate duties, let him fulfill them not displaying pomp here but eternal. Which, if they bring profit, let him repay equally praises and services to the eternal one. And although he may suffer eclipse, let him not, being without part, desert from demanding the same one, nor let him blaspheme in poverty, ignorant by what judgment he has been touched. Can unexpected changes not succeed? For if the vainglorious one will gape, grateful to be auctioned before himself, this one, crushed, will be able to be a hedgehog and to be thrust back frequently. Behold! But why do I, lavished with noise, like an emphatic herald, strive to explain the digested state of the departing age, whose diseases not even eloquent commentary can bear to cast forth? But worse, it once languished while it was sick, and pressed down by filth, redoubling lamentations, it was wasting away with salvation already exhausted. To whom also the first-called stood over to demolish with poisonous bites. But looking back meanwhile, he who had anointed the same one with his palm came to the aid of the worn one, and shook him from the mouth of the same one—whom also, while avoiding, he bit. And making firm again then the loosened and weakened world, he established it to stand.
chunks.notes: If given office, one should serve humbly for eternal reward, not earthly glory. Even when suffering adversity, one should not despair or blaspheme God but recognize reversals can come. The proud will fall. The author then shifts to allegory: the world itself once lay dying, poisoned by the first-called one (Satan), but Christ (the anointed one) rescued it from the serpent's mouth, crushed the serpent, and restored the world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cujus saluti pariter innotuit vitanda et utenda. Quod quia transgressus communicat noxiis, et recusat salutaria, hinc recidivare toties conspicitur. Sed et ipse opifex medicus et auctor salutis ingratis ponit medelam, nunc instanter oremus, ne adhuc motus furore jam persaepe impensis antidotis et pariter tensis lixopiritis retrahat manum vel premat chirurgiam, sed delegat unguenta a summis dinamidiis infundat et liniat quibus et repellat tabifica, ut cum venerit satio cirurgiae dilata his modicum inveniat quod praesumat exurere vel secare ne dicata thalamo Christi defleat praesetemina, quae lavit mystica inna-siterna. Nullus tam capax quibit esse peritus medicinae ni intente scrutetur physicam rationem. Et ne quis intelligit figmenti, ut qui ipsum finxerat. Quis ergo poterit tueri mundum salute? haud dicam Monarcham vel portionem in ipsa ni ipse figulus ejus primae plastis? Et si fateatur explici quodve per humanam formulam tamen almus Spiritus manet in ea quae ministrat inde, cui si pure famuletur in aevum ab illa devenient prospera quaeque fiant. Sceleribus inde citus et fugiet si commulgaverit. Quae deserta illico peribit tunc cum suis actibus ni eruderet se ab ipsis et consistat ut ante.
chunks.english: To whose salvation equally became known what should be avoided and what should be used. Because the transgressor communicates with harmful things and refuses salutary things, from this source it is seen to relapse so many times. But the craftsman himself, physician and author of salvation, places healing for the ungrateful. Now let us pray urgently that he not yet, moved by rage—with antidotes already very often dispensed and purging potions equally strained—withdraw his hand or press surgery, but may he delegate ointments from the highest powers, may he pour in and smear with which he may also repel destructive things, so that when the satiety of surgery delayed by these things comes, he may find little that he presumes to burn or cut away, lest the bride dedicated to Christ's chamber weep for the prior shoots which the mystical inner cistern washed. No one so capable will be able to be skilled in medicine unless he intently examines the physical principle. And lest anyone understand the fashioning except as he who had fashioned it. Who therefore will be able to protect the world with salvation? I will not say a monarch or a portion in it, unless he himself is its potter of the first molding? And if he confesses it to be unfolded, whatever through human form, nevertheless the kindly Spirit remains in that which she ministers from that source—to whom, if she serve purely into the age, from that will come prosperous things, whatever may be done. From crimes from there swiftly he will also flee if she will have milked together. Which, deserted, will immediately perish then with its actions, unless it should educate itself from them and stand as before.
chunks.notes: The world was taught what to avoid and embrace, but transgresses and rejects salvation, falling repeatedly. Yet God mercifully continues offering healing. The author prays God will not withdraw his medicinal hand in anger but continue applying ointments rather than resorting to the burning and cutting of final judgment, lest the Church (bride of Christ) be devastated. Only God, the craftsman who made the world, can truly heal it. The Holy Spirit works through created means, but if we serve faithfully, prosperity follows; if we fall into sin, blessing departs and destruction comes unless we repent and are restored.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde congruit, ut qui parat potiri nomine Augusti caveat hanc censuram, inlatum communi reto suscipiat; sic quoque vivat adepto ne temeraverit ipsum. In quo agat se divis nutibus, et frustretur suum sint arbitri illustres, qui adhuc superant, et qui jam praecesserant hinc et diathecam ponat speculum, quod nec reluat callidus caupo fraudolentus tabernarius aquam vino miscens, quod refertur etiam ad falsos doctores, qui vera falsis inficientes noxias solent propinare doctrinas; hi denique non secundum rei veritatem vel auctorum definitionem, sed propria adinventione sacras quaerunt tractare scripturas ut potentius sibi gratiam et populi inde favorem concilient. Quapropter jugiter devotus imploret Dominum, et non adjiciat comperendinare. Debitum est etiam ut cunctus populus instet hoc supplere ne seducatur ab ipso, quoniam vim praecipui testamenti luctatur abolere, qui stolidus negligit flagitare pro ope sui patroni. Praesto sum indifferens, orate nunc, jam respondebo vobis in lingua Adae ut fiat; et si mavultis auspicare me a summa majestate, ut deluat noxam de me placido lumine, quem et fingat sibi gratum, et acceptet fundendas preces, quas nec valeat impiare molestus inlex, nec mutilare linguam hiantis politam ea inspirante, quae etiam nec sufferat alibi dilabi meum cor inquam facile, sed merear exaudiri a pio auctore pietatis.
chunks.english: From which source it is fitting that whoever prepares to obtain the name of Augustus should beware this judgment—let him receive what has been brought into the common net. Thus also let him live, having obtained it, lest he profane it. In which let him conduct himself according to divine nods, and let him frustrate his own—let there be illustrious judges who still excel and who already have preceded. From this source also let him place a mirror as testament, which not even the cunning innkeeper, the fraudulent tavern-keeper mixing water with wine, may wash back—which is referred also to false teachers who, by infecting true things with false, are accustomed to serve up harmful teachings. These finally, not according to the truth of the matter or the definition of authorities, but by their own invention, seek to handle sacred Scriptures so that they may more powerfully reconcile grace for themselves and from that the favor of the people. For this reason, constantly devoted, let him implore the Lord and not add to delaying. It is also owed that the entire people should persist in fulfilling this, lest it be seduced by him—since he who foolishly neglects to demand help from his patron struggles to abolish the force of the principal testament. I am ready, indifferent. Pray now; I will now respond to you in Adam's tongue, so that it may be done. And if you prefer, take auspices for me from the highest majesty, so that he may wash away the fault from me with a placid light, whom also he may fashion for himself as pleasing, and may he accept the prayers to be poured forth—which neither may the troublesome enticer be able to make impious, nor to mutilate the polished tongue of one gaping, with that one inspiring who also may not allow my heart to slip away elsewhere, I say easily, but may I deserve to be heard by the pious author of piety.
chunks.notes: Therefore, whoever seeks imperial power must accept this judgment and the common good, living worthy of the office, submitting to divine guidance. Let him have wise counselors, living and from history. Let him establish a clear standard of truth, not like fraudulent innkeepers who water down wine—an image for false teachers who corrupt Scripture to win popularity. The ruler must pray constantly and not delay in seeking God. The people too must pray constantly lest they be deceived. The author concludes this section asking readers to pray for him, that God would cleanse him from sin, make him pleasing, accept his prayers, protect him from tempters, keep his heart fixed on God, and grant him to be heard by the merciful God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quo auxilio fretus oriar tunc dependere supplices voces quas confirmet vester Hebraicus succentus una dictione, et extremis ejus oris aptetur chronis.
chunks.english: Trusting in which help, I will then arise to pour forth suppliant words, which may your Hebrew chant confirm with one utterance, and may it be fitted with the final times of his speech.
chunks.notes: The work concludes with the author stating he will offer prayers which the readers should confirm with the Hebrew word 'Amen' (one utterance), fitting to the end times. This brings the complex, allegorical political-theological critique full circle back to prayer and eschatological awareness.
